critic_raw:
The methodology is carefully constructed and avoids several common pitfalls. User-level splits prevent cross-user contamination, the preprocessing pipeline is frozen before model fitting, labels are well-defined, and the scope is honestly bounded to a mature-user cohort. That said, I identify three genuine methodological concerns — not implementation nits — that could undermine the validity of the primary comparison.

---

**Issue 1: Preprocessing moments estimated from the assembled cohort, which includes validation and test users**

The methodology states: 'Continuous variables are standardized using a single set of moments estimated from the assembled cohort.' The assembled cohort is all users across the 36-month window before the train/val/test split is applied. This means the mean and standard deviation used to standardize features are computed using information from validation and test users.

Why this is wrong: For static or nearly-static features (tenure, lifetime plays, tier), leakage is minimal because test-user outcomes don't influence the moments meaningfully. But for behavioral features that vary over calendar time — weekly play counts, session durations, support contacts — the moments reflect distributional properties of the full 36-month cohort, including whatever behavior patterns characterized validation and test users during their observation windows. If churn-proximate behavior differs systematically from non-churn behavior (which it almost certainly does, since behavioral degradation precedes churn), the standardization moments are contaminated by label-adjacent information from held-out users. This is soft leakage: it doesn't directly encode outcomes, but it biases the feature space in a way that would not be available at genuine deployment time, where only training-cohort statistics would be known.

Evidence for or against: Compare model performance when moments are estimated only from training-split users versus the full cohort. If the gap is large, leakage is material. A cleaner test: check whether the training-split-only moments differ substantially from the full-cohort moments for the behavioral features most associated with churn (e.g., coefficient of variation in weekly plays). Large differences indicate non-trivial leakage.

---

**Issue 2: Temporal confounding from the fixed train/validation/test calendar structure**

The proposed model is trained on events from months 1–12 and evaluated on outcomes from months 13–36. The split is at the user level, but the calendar window is the same for all users. This means:

- Training features are always derived from months 1–12.
- Outcomes (churn or censoring) are always observed in months 13–36.

This is not random sampling from the 36-month observation period — it is a specific temporal regime. The model learns to predict churn in a specific 24-month calendar window (months 13–36) from behavior observed in a specific 12-month calendar window (months 1–12). The validation and test splits use identical calendar windows and therefore face identical macro-level conditions (platform growth rate, content catalog maturity, competitive landscape, pricing changes). The held-out users are not a temporal holdout; they are contemporaneous users who happened not to be assigned to training.

Why this matters: The experiment as designed cannot distinguish between (a) the sequence features capturing genuine behavioral signals that generalize across time, and (b) the sequence features capturing calendar-specific patterns (e.g., engagement dynamics during a particular content release cycle or promotional period that happens to co-occur with months 1–12 of the study). A model that memorizes 'users who watched heavily in Q2 of year 1 churn at rate X in the following two years' may look well-calibrated in this setup but fail entirely when deployed on users whose behavioral histories span a different calendar period.

The 0.05 IBS improvement threshold does not protect against this because both models are evaluated on the same temporally-confounded test set.

Evidence for or against: Hold out the final 6 months of the observation window (months 31–36) as a temporal test set separate from the user-level test split. If IBS degrades substantially on this temporal holdout relative to the user-level test split, temporal confounding is a real problem. A rigorous design would include at least one temporal out-of-sample evaluation period.

---

**Issue 3: Asymmetric hyperparameter treatment creates a comparison that is not against an optimized baseline**

The methodology explicitly states that the Cox baseline 'uses its standard regularization/proportional-hazards specification rather than a dedicated hyperparameter search.' The proposed Weibull sequence model undergoes 3-fold cross-validation hyperparameter tuning within the training split.

The stated justification is to 'preserve a clean operational reference.' This reasoning is circular: a 'clean operational reference' is only clean if the baseline is already well-specified, but that is precisely what hyperparameter tuning is meant to establish. Cox proportional hazards with regularization has at least one hyperparameter that matters materially — the regularization strength (L1 or L2 penalty magnitude) — and in high-dimensional settings (this baseline includes multiple marketing, calendar, and behavioral aggregates), the choice of penalty strength substantially affects both discrimination and calibration.

Why this matters specifically for IBS: The integrated Brier score penalizes miscalibration. A Cox model with suboptimal regularization will produce poorly calibrated survival curves even if its ranking (C-index) is reasonable. Since IBS is the primary metric and success is defined by a fixed 0.05 absolute improvement threshold, an under-tuned baseline artificially inflates the gap and makes the threshold easier to achieve. If the baseline were tuned to the same degree as the proposed model, the required 0.05 IBS improvement might shrink to zero or reverse.

Evidence for or against: Run the Cox baseline with a grid search over the regularization penalty (same 3-fold CV protocol, same stratification, same evaluation metric). Compare the tuned Cox IBS to the untuned Cox IBS. If the gap is larger than 0.05, the success criterion for the sequence model is already satisfied by better-tuning the baseline alone, which would mean the 0.05 threshold is not measuring the incremental value of sequence features at all.

---

**Summary**

Issues 1 and 3 are independently testable and could meaningfully affect the primary comparison. Issue 2 is structural and cannot be corrected without modifying the evaluation design — it limits the generalizability claim rather than falsifying the within-study result. None of these are implementation nits; all three are design-level choices with specific mechanisms of failure.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing standardization moments are estimated from the full assembled cohort (including validation and test users), creating soft leakage through label-adjacent behavioral statistics that would not be available at deployment time.

The fixed calendar mapping (features from months 1–12, outcomes from months 13–36) means all splits share the same temporal regime, preventing the evaluation from distinguishing genuine behavioral signals from calendar-specific patterns that may not generalize under deployment distribution shift.

The Cox baseline is intentionally left untuned while the proposed Weibull sequence model undergoes cross-validated hyperparameter selection, inflating the IBS gap and making the 0.05 absolute improvement threshold easier to satisfy without actually measuring the incremental value of sequence features.
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing standardization moments are estimated from the full assembled cohort (including validation and test users), creating soft leakage through label-adjacent behavioral statistics that would not be available at deployment time.

The fixed calendar mapping (features from months 1–12, outcomes from months 13–36) means all splits share the same temporal regime, preventing the evaluation from distinguishing genuine behavioral signals from calendar-specific patterns that may not generalize under deployment distribution shift.

The Cox baseline is intentionally left untuned while the proposed Weibull sequence model undergoes cross-validated hyperparameter selection, inflating the IBS gap and making the 0.05 absolute improvement threshold easier to satisfy without actually measuring the incremental value of sequence features.
